channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.eurogamer.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.eurogamer.net

item.title: Valve tackles "incredibly frustrating" sold-out Steam Controller shortage with new reservation system
item.content:encoded:
Couldn't get hold of one of Valve's pricey (£85) but fancy new Steam Controllers before they sold out? There's possibly a solution for you.

Overnight, Valve announced a reservation programme for the Steam Controller, which will begin today, 8th May, at 10am Pacific Time (6pm UK time). This entitles you to essentially reserve your place in line to order a new Steam Controller when it comes back in stock. Note, there's no word on when the Steam Controller will be back in stock, and "replenishment of inventory will vary from region to region", Valve noted in a Steam update post.

Watch on YouTube

To stop scalpers reserving all of the Steam Controller stock and selling on eBay at higher prices again, there are some fairly strict limitations associated with the reservation programme. (Limitations it would have been nice to have in the first place.) These are:

  • One Steam Controller per user
  • No eligibility for people who already bought a Steam Controller (through Steam, presumably)
  • Three days or 72 hours to make your purchase on Steam once you receive the order email
  • Having a Steam account in "good standing"
  • Having an account that bought something on Steam before 27th April 2026

"Steam Controller launched on May 4th, and while we were happy to see such a high level of interest, the experience for a lot of you trying to buy it was incredibly frustrating," Valve noted in its post. "We plan to continue replenishing stock as we get more in, but in the meantime wanted to share changes we're making to improve the purchase experience and to limit reseller activity.

"Beginning May 8th at 10am Pacific, we're opening a reservation queue for Steam Controller. Once you reserve, your place in line will be saved. When we go back in stock, order emails will be sent in the same order that reservations were made."

Reservations will start being fulfilled next week in the US and Canada, and in "the following weeks" in the UK, Europe and Australia.

We spent a week testing Valve's new Steam Controller and declared it "better in almost every way" compared to the 2015 original Steam Controller. It's now rechargeable, has more input options, has better sticks and buttons and generally a more sturdy design, and of course the iconic mouse-control-enabling touchpad controls return, though we weren't entirely convinced by the new placement of these. "It's a rock-solid upgrade," Matt wrote. "But for everyone else, at £85, it's unlikely to be the best choice compared to traditional controllers."

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/steam-controller-valve-adds-reservations-system
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: PS5 sales expected to decline due to generative AI memory drought and rising prices
item.content:encoded:
Sony has released its financial results for the past fiscal year. Inside, it predicts a decrease in PS5 hardware sales during the next 12 months.

The report, which you can read in full on Sony's corporate site, details on page 13 how Sony anticipates a "decrease in unit sales" between April 2026 - 2027.

At the bottom of this page, Sony elaborates on why it has predicted a decrease in PS5 sales, writing: "We plan to base our PS5 hardware sales in FY26 on the volume of memory we can procure at reasonable prices and we expect hardware profitability to be essentially the same as FY25."

This memory shortage has been explicitly caused by the rise in generative AI technology, and has led to both price increases for gaming hardware as well as shortages in supply.

Valve recently blamed a shortage of Steam Decks on the AI boom, also announcing a delay for Steam Machines for similar AI reasons. This led Sony to reportedly consider a PS6 price hike, with Nintendo announcing a price hike for the Switch 2 in the US only today.

This problem is not expected to go away soon. Apple expects supply issues to continue into the foreseeable future, and new Xbox CEO Asha Sharma has hinted that memory costs could impact pricing and availability of next-gen consoles.

If you want some sugar to help this particularly bitter news go down, Sony - on the same slide - predicted an increase in first-party game sales this fiscal year. So, if you have a PS5, one hopes you'll at least have some cool stuff to play on it. If you can afford it in the first place.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/playstation-5-sales-decline-memory-ai
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Resident Evil Requiem's new game mode is here, free to download, and it looks like bloody chaos
item.content:encoded:
Resident Evil Requiem has a new game mode, which is out now for free for all those who have made it through the main story and want to keep flexing their survival muscles.

It's known as Leon Must Die Forever, and is as chill as you would imagine. Remember last month when Requiem's director Koshi Nakanishi said we should all 'sharpen our tomahawks' in anticipation? Well, I can see why he said that now.

"Unlocked by completing the story, this additional minigame mode challenges you to clear numerous stages as Leon while unlocking exclusive enhancer abilities," reads the official blurb for Leon Must Die Forever, with Capcom promising "fast-paced action" which is "distinct" from Resident Evil Requiem's main story mode.

The studio added Leon Must Die Forever is "packed" with replay value. "Can you make it to the end alive and defeat the boss within the time limit?" the developer asks. Well, knowing my rather chaotic gameplay style, probably not, no.

But, if you are much more competent than me, and already think this all sounds like a walk in the park, Capcom has also added a super duper difficulty level, which it says is only for Requiem's most confident players, so sorry Grace, I don't think this one will be for you. As for what this all means, all Capcom has said is "True to the title, you'll 'die forever'..." Gulp.

You can check out the trailer for Resident Evil Requiem's Leon Must Die Forever mode below. Also, is it just me that thinks this could be a reference to the now-classic Devil May Cry mode 'Dante Must Die'? A nice little internal Capcom reference, maybe?

As well as this new game mode, Capcom has also fixed an issue which was causing Resident Evil Requiem to become unplayable under certain circumstances as part of this update.

If you haven't given Resident Evil Requiem a go yet, I would thoroughly recommend it, but don't just take my word for it. In Eurogamer's Resident Evil Requiem review, our Matt also showered the game with praise, declaring it an "all-time high" for the long-running horror franchise.

Since its release, Requiem has been a huge success for Capcom, earning itself the title as the fastest-selling game in the Resident Evil series to date. More content in the form of a new story expansion, which will "delve deeper into the world of Requiem", is also on the cards, though we don't have a release date yet.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/resident-evil-requiem-leon-must-die-forver-game-mode
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Nintendo announces Switch 2 price increase, consoles will soon be $500 in the US
item.content:encoded:
"We sincerely apologise for the impact these price revisions may have on our customers."

Nintendo has announced a price increase for the Switch 2. This follows reports Nintendo was facing pressure from investors to increase the price of its Switch 2 console, due to concerns about its profitability.

These increases will impact consumers in the US, Canada, Japan and Europe. In the US, Canada and Europe, Switch 2 price increases will come into effect from 1st September, with the changes listed below:

  • US - from $449.99 to $499.99
  • Canada - from $629.99 to $679.99
  • Europe - from €469.99 to €499.99

In Japan, meanwhile, the price of the Switch 2 will increase from ¥49,980 to ¥59,980 from 25th May. The country will also see an increase in the price of the original Switch's family of consoles, as well its Nintendo Switch Online subscription, in order to "support appropriate alignment among regions".

Nintendo cited "changes in market conditions" as well as consideration of its "global business outlook" as the reason for these price increases. "We sincerely apologise for the impact these price revisions may have on our customers and other stakeholders, and we deeply appreciate your understanding," the company said.

Nintendo is not the only company to have raised the price of its hardware in recent months. In March of this year, Sony announced significant price increases for its PlayStation range, which saw the cost of a base PS5 climb by around £90 in the UK. At this time, industry analyst and research director at Ampere Analysis Piers Harding-Rolls told Eurogamer it was likely that both Microsoft and Nintendo would follow suit, and increase their own prices in the near future. And now, Nintendo has for many regions.

Meanwhile, in April, head of Xbox Asha Sharma admitted the current price of RAM could lead to a price bump and limited stock where its next-gen console, Project Helix, is concerned.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/nintendo-switch-2-price-increase-may-2025
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
